coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-based hydrogel microspheres for sensitive detection of caffeic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongyuan
Wang, Jieqiong
Han, Ce
coredata.dc:description: In this investigation, blue-emitting graphene quantum dots (B,N-GQDs) were synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal method in an ethanol/water mixture utilizing 3-APBA as the nitrogen and boron sources. These B,N-GQDs showcased a highly graphitized structure, complemented by surface-functionalized boronic acid and amino groups that bestowed upon them remarkable optical attributes and water solubility. Leveraging the interplay of internal filtering and dynamic quenching effects, the sensor demonstrated remarkable selectivity and sensitivity in detecting caffeic acid (CA) within the range of 0–840 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.63 μM. Moreover, the fluorescent gel microspheres engineered with B,N-GQDs exhibited robust CA sensing capabilities and portability. As such, the application potential of B,N-GQDs expands to encompass drug metabolism monitoring, alongside the assessment of food and environmental safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Graphene quantum dot
Caffeic acid (CA)
Portable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of B,N-GQDs and optimization of preparation conditions
CA detection based on fluorescent gel microspheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of B,N-GQDs, inset showing lattice image; (b) average particle size distribution; (c) Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) 3D image; and (d) AFM plane image, inset shows the height distribution of B,N-GQDs underlined.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of B,N-GQDs solution in the presence of CA; (b) Linear fitting between the fluorescence intensity ratio of B,N-GQDs before and after adding different concentrations of CA.
Visual detection of CA by B, N-GQDs (a) Macroscopic picture of fluorescence changes; (b) Color analysis curves of changes in R, G, and B values with CA concentration; (c) Linear fitting curve.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of GQDs and its portable detection of CA.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-based hydrogel microspheres for sensitive detection of caffeic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Jilin Province Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Chemical Power, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry
Jilin Province Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Chemical Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Jilin Province Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Chemical Power, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this investigation, blue-emitting graphene quantum dots (B,N-GQDs) were synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal method in an ethanol/water mixture utilizing 3-APBA as the nitrogen and boron sources. These B,N-GQDs showcased a highly graphitized structure, complemented by surface-functionalized boronic acid and amino groups that bestowed upon them remarkable optical attributes and water solubility. Leveraging the interplay of internal filtering and dynamic quenching effects, the sensor demonstrated remarkable selectivity and sensitivity in detecting caffeic acid (CA) within the range of 0–840 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.63 μM. Moreover, the fluorescent gel microspheres engineered with B,N-GQDs exhibited robust CA sensing capabilities and portability. As such, the application potential of B,N-GQDs expands to encompass drug metabolism monitoring, alongside the assessment of food and environmental safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Caffeic acid (CA) is a prominent phenolic acid found in plants and has potential medical applications as a hemostatic agent, anti-tumor agent, and HIV inhibitor [1]. The development of a precise and sensitive CA detection method holds considerable research importance, especially. In recent years, fluorescence detection has garnered substantial interest due to its straightforward operational procedures and high precision. The remarkable optical properties, excellent water solubility, broad tunability, ease of surface functionalization, and biocompatibility of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn increasing attention. Zhang et al. recently reported a colorimetric logic detection platform for Fe3+ and ATP using N-GQDs [2].

While a number of techniques exist for detecting CA, including high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, ultraviolet spectrophotometry, and electrochemical techniques, GQDs remain relatively underused [3,4]. Due to their challenging recyclability traits, GQDs are vulnerable to secondary contamination, curtailing their practical use [5]. GQDs were successfully integrated into sodium alginate and calcium chloride fluorescent gel microspheres in this study to serve as sensing units. A visualized, portable detection of CA was enabled by the changes in their fluorescence. Furthermore, these fluorescent gel microspheres exhibit CA adsorption ability, thus constructing a versatile portable sensor (Scheme 1 ).
Materials and reagents section, instrumentation details and determination of caffeic acid detection and adsorption properties were provided in the ESI (see electronic supplementary information).

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize B,N-GQDs. 3-APBA was added to ethanol and water solution (20 mL 10:90 v:v). At 200 °C, it was heated for 24 h in a 35 mL polytetrafluoroethylene container. The resultant solution was centrifuged for 25 min. After dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular cutoff of 300 Da, B,N-GQDs were dialyzed for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B,N-GQDs and optimization of preparation conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The dosage of 3-APBA and ethanol has little effect on the fluorescence emission of the product (Fig. S1a, b). Additionally, replacing ethanol with another solvent cannot achieve the expected fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. S1c). When temperature and reaction time were changed, fluorescence emission intensity of B,N-GQDs decreased. According to Fig. S1d and e, this reduction occurs due to molecular copolymerization and excessive carbonization.

B,N-GQDs showed good dispersion and were close to spherical shape (Fig. 1 a), with average sizes of 4.79 ± 0.42 nm and 7.03 ± 0.66 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). These dimensions are consistent with the (002) crystal plane of the graphite phase. The height range was confirmed by AFM observation from 4.79 nm to 8.41 nm, consistent with the TEM results (Fig. 1c, d).

The diffraction peak of the B,N-GQDs was observed around 2θ = 18.66°, indexed as (002), closely resembling JCPDS card No. 47–0787 (Fig. S2a). The Raman spectrum at 1347 cm−1 (D band) and 1572 cm−1 (G band) indicated highly graphitized structures and sp2 hybridized carbon atom defects in the B,N-GQDs (Fig. S2b). N-H, B-O, and C-B bonds are unambiguously manifested in the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, confirming successful doping of B and N atoms (Fig. S2a) [6]. The presence of amino and boronic acid groups on the surface of the B,N-GQDs was confirmed. In the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. S3a), graphitic nitrogen (398.53 eV), pyridine/pyrrole nitrogen (398.98 eV), and amino nitrogen (399.42 eV) were detected in addition to the existence of B-N, B-C, and B-O bonds (284.01 eV, 284.35 eV, and 284.72 eV, respectively) within the B,N-GQDs (Fig. S3b-f). This outcome aligns with the FT-IR findings, confirming the successful incorporation of B and N atoms and the effective modification involving −B(OH)2 and –NH2 groups [7].

B,N-GQDs exhibit distinct absorption bands at 282 nm and 316–398 nm, representing n-π* and π-π* transitions, respectively. It was found that 322 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength, and 407 nm the maximum emission wavelength. Fig. S3b illustrates B,N-GQDs with excitation-independent traits between 302–347 nm. Ultimately, the absolute quantum yield of B,N-GQDs was measured at 31.7 %.

For stability assessment, B,N-GQDs were used as a reference. B,N-GQDs maintained steady fluorescence emission intensity over 80 % between 35 °C and 65 °C (Fig. S4a). Fluorescence intensity was significantly diminished (Fig. S4b) when pH was below 4 or above 11. B,N-GQDs interact with positively charged ammonium ions through protonation of partial ammonium ions (NH3+) and boric acid diprotonates (B(OH)4−). Under strongly alkaline conditions, amino radicals (NH2) and borate anions (B(OH)3−) impacted fluorescence emission. NaCl tolerance and photobleaching had minimal effects on fluorescence intensity (Fig. S4c, d). This indicates that B,N-GQDs may be useful for detecting CA in blood, urine, and environmental samples.

Our study compared B,N-GQDs with CA, metal ions, and biological small molecules. Only Fe3+ was found to interfere with B,N-GQDs (Fig. S5), and EDTA was used as a masking agent. Then, we optimized detection parameters (Fig. S6). As an optimized condition, we used 2 min of reaction time, 0.5 mg of B,N-GQDs, and pH 7.

Under optimal detection conditions, the fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0 of B,N-GQDs is linearly related to the concentration of CA in the range of 0–840 μM (y = 0.00094x + 0.03) (R2 = 0.9964). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.63 μM using 3σ/K (Fig. 2 ). Notably, this method exhibits a wider linear range compared to other CA detection techniques (Table S1).

In the exploration of the CA detection mechanism by B,N-GQDs, the Stern-Volmer equation and its variations (Eq. (1) were generally considered the most suitable methods for elucidating the quenching mechanism:
(1)

k q

K a
Q

According to Fig. S7a, there was a double-exponential relationship between fluorescence intensity and CA concentration, along with a clear spectral overlap between B,N-GQD excitation and CA absorption (Fig. S7b, c). In Park's study, the Fco/Fobsd value also increased with CA concentration (from 1.72 to 1.85). Ultimaly，dynamic quenching effect was also demonstrated with B,N-GQDs as their fluorescence lifetime decreased from 8.24 ns to 7.57 ns upon CA addition (Fig. S7d). CA quenched B,N-GQD fluorescence through a synergistic effect resulting from both inner filtering and dynamic quenching [8].
(2)

e m

We evaluated the applicability of the proposed method in human urine, mouse serum, and coffee beverages. CA recovery rates were between 96.72 % and 110.00 %. B,N-GQDs consistently maintained a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.45, demonstrating their efficacy at detecting CA. According to Table S3, B,N-GQDs' reproducibility and stability in functional sensing are very good. Their precision is within 1.12 % to 3.74 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA detection based on fluorescent gel microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: We used fluorescent gel microspheres to detect CA in aqueous solutions. Fluorescence quenching was observed after submerging fluorescent gel microspheres for 20 min in varying CA concentration solutions (Fig. 3 a). In addition, a portable CA detection platform was developed using UV light and the “ColorDesk” smartphone application. As shown in Table S4, the R value decreased gradually with increasing CA concentration, while the G value increased incrementally. Linear regression indicated y = -7.429x + 1.314 (R2 = 0.992), demonstrating the capability of fluorescent gel microspheres anchored with B,N-GQDs to facilitate portable CA sensing (Fig. 3b).

In this investigation, B,N-GQDs were synthesized via a solvothermal approach to detect CA in blood, urine, and coffee beverage samples. By integrating the color analysis of B,N-GQD-incorporated alginate gel microspheres, this study substantiated the robust functionality of this portable sensor. Thus, the research advances a precise and portable strategy for CA detection, indicating the promise of expanding the utilization of GQDs as versatile fluorescence sensors.

I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

Hongyuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jieqiong Wang: Supervision, Data curation. Ce Han: Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22102172).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136481.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
